title: A Visual Proof That One Layer Can Compute Anything
tagline: Flatten sigmoids into steps, glue bumps, stack towers — Nielsen's visual proof of universality, made interactive
summary: Rebuild Michael Nielsen's visual proof of the Universal Approximation Theorem by hand: crank a sigmoid into a step, subtract two steps into a bump, stack bumps to design a 1D curve, then build a tower step-by-step and tile towers across a rotatable 3D surface.
highlights: Watch a sigmoid snap into a step function and learn Nielsen's reparametrization by step position s = -b/w.
Subtract two steps to make a bump, then design a whole function with a bank of per-bump height sliders — including the σ⁻¹∘f twist the output sigmoid hides.
Build a 2D tower step-by-step (wall, ridge, cross, threshold) and tile towers across a rotatable 3D surface to approximate any function, watching the skyline sharpen with resolution.
